questions.question: What is an EtherChannel?
options: A bundle of multiple physical interfaces grouped into a single logical interface for increased bandwidth and redundancy
questions.explanation: EtherChannel (also called Port Channel or LAG) is a bundle of multiple physical interfaces grouped into a single logical interface for increased bandwidth and redundancy.

questions.question: Which EtherChannel negotiation protocol is an IEEE industry standard?
questions.explanation: LACP (Link Aggregation Control Protocol) is the industry-standard EtherChannel negotiation protocol (IEEE 802.3ad). PAgP is Cisco-proprietary.

questions.question: Which LACP mode pair does NOT form a channel?
questions.explanation: Valid LACP combinations: active/active or active/passive form a channel; passive/passive does not.

questions.question: Which PAgP mode pair does NOT form a channel?
questions.explanation: Valid PAgP combinations: desirable/desirable or desirable/auto form a channel; auto/auto does not.

questions.question: Which command configures static EtherChannel with no negotiation?
questions.explanation: `channel-group 1 mode on` creates a static EtherChannel with no negotiation protocol.

questions.question: What is the LACP 16-port rule?
options: LACP allows up to 16 ports in a group, but only 8 active at a time; others are hot standby
A maximum of 16 LACP channels per switch
questions.explanation: LACP 16-port rule: LACP allows up to 16 ports in a group, but only 8 active at a time; the others are hot standby.

questions.question: What is a 'flow' in EtherChannel load-balancing?
options: A communication between two endpoints; frames in one flow always use the same physical link
A single frame sent across the channel
A random hash of all packet headers
The total bandwidth of the channel
questions.explanation: A flow is a communication between two endpoints; EtherChannel load-balances at the flow level so frames in one flow always use the same physical link.

questions.question: Which attributes must match on all member interfaces of an EtherChannel?
options: Speed, duplex, switchport mode, allowed VLANs, and native VLAN
questions.explanation: All member interfaces must match in speed, duplex, switchport mode, allowed VLANs, and native VLAN.

questions.question: In `show etherchannel summary`, what does flag 'P' mean?
questions.explanation: Flags in `show etherchannel summary`: S = L2, R = L3 routed, U = in use, P = bundled, D = down, s = suspended.

questions.question: What does LAG stand for?
questions.explanation: LAG (Link Aggregation Group) is another name for EtherChannel, used especially with WLCs.

questions.question: What is oversubscription?
options: When total host-facing bandwidth exceeds the uplink bandwidth
When a port is assigned to more than one VLAN
When PAgP and LACP are both enabled
When the EtherChannel has more than 8 active links
questions.explanation: Oversubscription occurs when total host-facing bandwidth exceeds the uplink bandwidth; some oversubscription is acceptable, too much causes congestion.

questions.question: What does the command `channel-group 1 mode passive` do?
options: LACP passive; only forms a channel if the other side is active
PAgP passive; only forms if other side is desirable
questions.explanation: `channel-group 1 mode passive` is LACP passive; only forms if other side is active.
